questions.text: A photograph from the past can plunge me back into the feelings of that era with startling force.
I can tell when someone is smiling with their mouth but not with the muscles around their eyes.
I find that my emotional responses are predictable — I can usually anticipate how I'll feel about something.
I have a strong desire to leave my mark on the things I work on.
I sometimes feel like my nervous system has no middle gear — it's either idling or redlining.
I am the type who contributes thoughtfully to an agenda someone else has set.
I feel that when suffering is present, its moral claim on me is immediate and does not require further justification.
I am the type who creates the rules of engagement for a group.
I outline chapters or sections before I begin writing anything substantial.
I believe that the clearest path through any emotionally complex situation is to focus on what can be known and done rather than on what is felt.
I sense when two people are communicating through a third person who does not realize they are being used as a channel.
I feel that my job is to support the group in finding its own way.
I believe that approaching emotionally complex situations with analytical rigor is the most reliable way to ensure fair treatment for everyone.
I feel that the process of planning itself helps me understand what I'm trying to achieve.
I tend to respond to every request for help before attending to my own inbox.
I align daily activities with my long-term vision so every day moves me forward.
I find that my emotional state changes gradually, like a thermostat rather than a switch.
I find that other people's gratitude means more to me than any personal reward.
I tend to allocate the majority of my time to tasks that benefit other people.
I hold the view that my own intellectual curiosity should guide how I spend my learning time.
I prefer to set the timeline for a group effort rather than negotiate it.
I notice that I cannot easily mask my emotional state because my body broadcasts it so loudly.
I find that analytical clarity enables me to untangle complex situations where suffering has multiple causes and no simple solution.
I do not pay much attention to facial expressions during conversations.
I prefer that high-stakes elements be handled by whoever the group deems best suited.
I find that I'm most effective when my own life is in good order.
I approach repeated tasks with fresh eyes each time rather than standardizing them.
I like to try a different approach each time, even if the old way worked.
I often sense that there is a conversation happening beneath the surface conversation.
I feel that no quantity of reasoning can legitimately override the moral demand that arises from directly knowing of another's pain.
I believe that accumulating experiences that enrich my life is a valid use of resources.
I find it hard to detect when someone is being supportive on the surface but competitive underneath.
I do not pick up on when a person gives a long explanation because they sense skepticism from the listener.
I believe that suffering, wherever it occurs, creates moral claims that are binding regardless of personal convenience.
I tend to feel the same basic emotional temperature whether I'm alone or in a crowd.
I deal with maintenance needs when they become apparent rather than on a schedule.
